title: Design LLM-Native Interface
prompt: Design an LLM-native MCP interface for a WordPress plugin that teaches a technical skill. Include orientation, initialize instructions, public tools, resources, stable handles, next_actions or tool_calls, memory retrieval, completion certificates, error recovery, and private boundaries.
hints: The model should not need to infer the workflow from a tool pile. Hand it the next useful move.

model_answer.summary: An LLM-native teaching interface should reduce inference by returning orientation, stable handles, exact next actions, memory retrieval, and clear public/private boundaries.
model_answer.work: Put the first move in initialize instructions: call begin-course. Public tools should include begin-course, get-study-plan, get-syllabus, search-course, get-lesson, get-exercise, attempt-exercise, get-next-work, get-progress, get-learning-memory, get-certificate, submit-feedback, and get-course-improvement-signals. Resources should expose syllabus and references for retrieval. Every response should repeat stable handles such as enrollment_key, lesson_slug, and exercise_slug when they are needed, and return next_actions or tool_calls with exact tool names and arguments. Completion should be explicit: when get-next-work reports complete, the LLM calls get-certificate to receive an anonymous certificate ID, verification code, and transcript. Missing handles, invalid slugs, oversized payloads, and rate limits should return WP_Error with recoverable messages. Authoring tools and private writes should stay hidden by default and require capability or bearer-token authorization if enabled.
checks: Verify initialize instructions name begin-course and the public study loop.
Check tool schemas and output schemas include stable handles, next_actions, and get-certificate completion metadata.
Test missing enrollment_key, invalid slug, oversized answer, and rate limit recovery.
Review public tools for secret leakage and private boundary violations.
Run php -l, course-flow simulation, course lab, and Plugin Check.
model_answer.why_this_is_good: It designs for how models actually operate: limited context, no guaranteed durable memory, and a need for exact tool calls instead of hidden workflow inference.
common_misses: Only exposing a tool list without an operating loop.
Returning prose directions but no exact next_actions.
Mixing public learner tools with private authoring tools.
model_answer.revision_prompt: Revise by adding the first call, stable handles, exact next tool calls, memory recovery, get-certificate completion, WP_Error recovery, and private boundaries.

summary.description: One or two sentences explaining the answer.

work.description: The main implementation, review, or design answer.

checks.description: Verification steps, review checks, or acceptance criteria.
